note: Just-in-time grammar notes. Each note surfaces in the reader when one of its `triggers` (glossary lemmas) appears in the passage, or the item id is in `items`. Comprehension aids — not drills. Body uses simple **markdown** bold.

notes.title: The words for “the”
notes.body: Italian has several words for **the**. Masculine: **il** (il libro), **lo** before s+consonant/z/gn (lo studente), **l'** before a vowel (l'amico); plural **i** / **gli**. Feminine: **la** (la casa), **l'** (l'amica); plural **le**.

notes.title: a / an
notes.body: “A/an” is **un / uno** (masculine) or **una / un'** (feminine): un libro, uno studente, una casa, un'amica. Use **uno** before s+consonant, z, gn, ps.

notes.title: Nouns have a gender
notes.body: Every noun is masculine or feminine. Most ending in **-o** are masculine (il libr**o**), most in **-a** are feminine (la cas**a**). Nouns in **-e** can be either (il fiore, la notte) — learn each with its article.

notes.title: Present tense: -are verbs
notes.body: Regular **-are** verbs (parlare): io parl**o**, tu parl**i**, lui/lei parl**a**, noi parl**iamo**, voi parl**ate**, loro parl**ano**.

notes.title: Present tense: -ere / -ire verbs
notes.body: **-ere** (prendere): prend-**o**, -**i**, -**e**, -**iamo**, -**ete**, -**ono**. **-ire** (dormire): dorm-**o**, -**i**, -**e**, -**iamo**, -**ite**, -**ono**. Some -ire verbs insert **-isc-** (capire → cap**isc**o).

notes.title: essere vs stare
notes.body: **essere** = to be (who/what you are): sono italiano. **stare** = to stay, or how you feel: come **stai**? — sto bene. Use **stare** for health and feelings.

notes.title: avere — to have
notes.body: **avere**: io **ho**, tu **hai**, lui/lei **ha**, noi **abbiamo**, voi **avete**, loro **hanno**. The **h** is always silent. Italian also uses avere for age: **ho** trent'anni (I'm 30).

notes.title: Little words: a, in, di, da
notes.body: **a** = to/at (a Roma), **in** = in/to (in Italia), **di** = of/from (di Maria), **da** = from/by (da Roma). They fuse with the article: a+il = **al**, di+il = **del**, in+la = **nella**.

notes.title: Asking questions
notes.body: Question words: **chi** (who), **cosa / che** (what), **dove** (where), **quando** (when), **perché** (why), **come** (how), **quanto** (how much). Word order stays like a statement — the voice just rises: Vuoi un caffè?

notes.title: Adjectives agree
notes.body: An adjective matches its noun in gender and number: il gatto ner**o**, la casa ner**a**, i gatti ner**i**, le case ner**e**. Adjectives usually come **after** the noun.

notes.title: Talking about the past
notes.body: The **passato prossimo** = **avere/essere** (present) + past participle: **ho** mangiat**o** (I ate), **sono** andat**o** (I went). Most verbs take **avere**; verbs of movement/change take **essere**, and then the participle agrees: sono andat**a** (a woman speaking).
